data.title: Gavin Newsom, Dems avoid questions about trans athletes in sports
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom can’t handle the truth — or at least isn’t able to articulate what it is. Here he was flubbing a very basic and fundamental question from Ben Shapiro about whether men can become women. 

He’s not the only one, of course. Most Democrats, including most of the potential 2028 contenders, do not want to answer even easy questions about transgender issues. Axios tried to get them on the record on whether trans girls should compete against biological girls in women’s sports, and most people dodged the question.  

Let’s be specific. The list of Democrats who would not even bother responding to that simple question is as follows: Kamala Harris, Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, JB Pritzker, Cory Booker, Andy Beshear, Chris Murphy, Ro Khanna and Newsom. 

The ones who did respond were Josh Shapiro, Pete Buttigieg and Rahm Emanuel. Shapiro said he didn’t want trans athletes to have an unfair advantage; Emanuel said, flatly, no, they shouldn’t compete; and Buttigieg said this was largely a question for sports leagues, not the government. Credit to them for even responding — most Democrats don’t want to admit that they either agree with their radical progressive base on this issue, or (more likely) are afraid of offending their radical progressive base. 

Trans people, of course, deserve dignity, and compassion and equal treatment. The government should not interfere with private medical decisions made by consenting adults. But with children it’s different, and we all broadly accept that the state does take an interest in determining what medical care is appropriate for minors. 

And then when we get to youth sports, and schools, and those accommodations, it is obvious that we need sane policies — policies that the vast majority of Americans agree with, which put commonsense limitations in place. We cannot have a teenage boy declare himself a girl and suddenly gain access to the girl’s locker room or inclusion on the girls’ swim team. That would be completely ridiculous. And if Democrats do not realize that is ridiculous, and more importantly, admit that this is ridiculous, they are going to lose elections. 

But don’t tell Tim Miller that! The Bulwark commentator responded to the Axios inquiry on X, writing: “‘Can a man become a woman’ is such a rage bait BS question. It has no bearing on policy. In a free country an adult can become whatever they want if they don’t harm others. The DHS Secretary has an entirely new face & the president wears more makeup than most drag queens.” 

Uh, OK, but wearing a lot of makeup does not magically transform a man into a woman, and a face lift doesn’t change a woman into a man. That’s kind of the whole point of this debate: that the fundamental truth of a person’s biology remains constant despite surface-level cosmetic changes, even very invasive ones. Or at least, that’s what most people believe! If you feel differently, Democratic presidential aspirants, well, you’re welcome to say so.  

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Trump to Carney: 'Canada lives because of the United States'
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday sent a message to Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, telling him that his country “lives because of the United States,” days after the prime minister defended Greenland against Trump’s effort to acquire the island territory.

“We’re building a Golden Dome that’s going to, just by its very nature, going to be defending Canada,” he said during his speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. “Canada gets a lot of freebies from us, by the way. They should be grateful also, but they’re not. I watched your prime minister yesterday, he wasn’t so grateful.”

“But they should be grateful to us, Canada,” the president continued. “Canada lives because of the United States. Remember that, Mark, before you make your statements.”

Carney on Tuesday said Canada “strongly opposes” the Trump administration’s announcement of new tariffs on Denmark and several other European allies starting Feb. 1.

Trump, in announcing the import taxes, said they would remain “until such time as a Deal is reached for the Complete and Total purchase of Greenland.”

The Canadian leader, in remarks from Davos, doubled down on his support for the Arctic island’s sovereignty and the importance of supporting the NATO alliance.

“We stand firmly with Greenland in Denmark and fully support their unique right to determine Greenland’s future,” he said. “Our commitment to NATO’s Article 5 is unwavering, so we’re working with our NATO allies, including the Nordic-Baltic Eight to further secure the alliance’s northern and western flanks, including through Canada’s unprecedented investments in over-the-horizon radar, in submarines and aircraft and boots on the ground — boots on the ice.”

The president on Wednesday Trump pulled back from his threat to use military force to acquire Greenland. He praised the mineral-rich island and requested immediate negotiations over its future.

Trump also accused Denmark of being “ungrateful” and said the country would be “speaking German and a little Japanese, perhaps,” without the help of the U.S.

The remarks drew criticism from two Democratic governors — who have both been floated as possible 2028 presidential contenders. The president name-dropped California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D), calling him a “good guy” and that he “used to get along so great with Gavin when I was president.”

Newsom called the speech “remarkably boring” and said Trump, “was never going to invade Greenland; it was never real.”

“A lot of stuff stands out,” the California governor added. “None of this is normalized. There’s a normalization, a deviancy of consciousness and comments and commentary. He’s ahead of the curve, he’s graded on the curve. That’s really some jaw-dropping and remarkable statements that just fly in the face of facts and evidence and common sense.”

Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear (D) also attended the forum and called the president’s speech “dangerous, disrespectful and unhinged.”

“From insulting our allies to telling struggling Americans that he’s fixed inflation and the economy is amazing, the President is hurting both our families’ financial security and our national security,” Beshear wrote in a statement he shared on social platform X. “Oh, and Greenland is so important he’s calling it Iceland.”

data.title: Trump says NATO secretary general is 'more important' than Danish foreign minister
data.description:
President Trump told reporters on Wednesday that NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte is “more important” than Danish Foreign Minister Lars Løkke Rasmussen when asked about Danish officials’ unwillingness to discuss the U.S. acquiring Greenland. 

“They didn’t tell me that, so when they tell me because I don’t like getting it second hand. And if he wants to tell me, he’ll tell me right to my face,” Trump responded. 

When asked when he will be discussing the possible acquisition, Trump said he has “no idea.” 

“I’ll be discussing it with this man right here,” Trump said, pointing to Rutte. “He’s frankly more important.” 

Rasmussen addressed reporters following Trump’s speech at the World Economic Forum in Switzerland, in which he reiterated his goal to acquire Greenland but ruled out using military force. 

“What is quite clear is after this speech, the president’s ambition remains intact,” Rasmussen said, calling Trump’s statement that he will not use military force “positive.” “But that does not make this issue go away because it is being expressed very clearly that it is better to own than to rent, and that Denmark cannot look after Greenland.”

“We stand on a foundation that the United States itself largely helped create after World War Two where there are international legal rules where one must respect the people’s right to self-determination, the sovereignty of nations,” Rasmussen added. “And that’s how we enter these discussions, and we’re not going to budge an inch on that.” 

Earlier Wednesday, Trump made headlines when he said he would not use military force to acquire the Danish territory, but continued to argue in favor of the U.S. owning it.

“We probably won’t get anything unless I decide to use excessive strength and force where we would be frankly unstoppable. But, I won’t do that,” Trump said in Davos, speaking to the economic forum.

“I don’t have to use force. I don’t want to use force. I won’t use force. All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland,” he continued.

The president went on to lay out the argument for the U.S. acquiring the Danish territory, saying it would only “strengthen” the NATO alliance. 

Trump also knocked Denmark, calling the country “ungrateful” after the U.S. helped the country during World War II.

“Without us, you’d all be speaking German and a little Japanese, perhaps,” the president said. 

Trump called for immediate negotiations for the U.S. to acquire Greenland, reiterating his stance that the island isn’t adequately protected.

data.title: Newsom denied entry to USA House at Davos forum
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) was denied entry to the USA House in Davos, Switzerland, where he was invited to speak during the World Economic Forum.

“Under pressure from the White House and State Department, USA House (a church acting as the official US pavilion) is now denying entry to @CAGovernor Gavin Newsom to speak with media after Fortune — the official media partner — invited him to speak,” his press office wrote in a statement on the social platform X.

Fortune did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment. However, the outlet told Politico it did not succumb to political pressure and shared that the governor was still welcome at the venue.

Other sponsors include McKinsey & Company, Microsoft and C3 AI. 

USA House is celebrating the 250th anniversary of the United States in Davos with a series of speakers at “a small number of marquee venues along the Promenade, including penthouses and historic locations inside and adjacent to the security zone,” according to the USA House website.

Earlier this morning, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent attended a press briefing hosted by USA House where he criticized Newsom, a potential 2028 White House contender.

“Governor Newsom, who strikes me as Patrick Bateman meets Sparkle Beach Ken, may be the only Californian who knows less about economics than Kamala Harris…,” Bessent said.

“I was told he was asked to give a speech on his signature policies, but he’s not speaking.  Because what have his economic policies brought? Outward migration from California, a gigantic budget deficit,” Bessent quipped about Newsom.

“The largest homeless population in America, and the poor folks in the palisades who had their homes burned down,” the Treasury chief continued. “He is here hobnobbing with the global elite while his California citizens are still homeless.”

Bessent later added that the governor was “too smug, too self-absorbed, and too economically illiterate to know anything.”

Newsom has railed against the Trump administration since the start of his second term and slammed the leader during remarks to the press at Davos.

The Democrat urged American allies to “stand united” against the White House pressures to influence foreign policy and global political norms.

data.title: 'Kennedy' on Netflix: Here's who's starring in the historical drama
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Netflix has officially announced the rest of the cast of its new historical drama “Kennedy,” starring Michael Fassbender as the head of the dynasty.

Adapted from Fredrik Logevall’s book “JFK: Coming of Age in the American Century, 1917–1956,” the show will follow the life and legacy of one of the most famous and powerful dynasties in American history.

“Kennedy” showrunner Sam Shaw said in a statement to Netflix:

“The story of the Kennedys is the closest we have to American mythology — somewhere between Shakespeare and ‘The Bold and the Beautiful.’ But Fredrik Logevall’s stunning, nuanced biography pulls a veil on the human strivings and burdens behind the myth, revealing as much about our present moment, how we got here and where we’re going, as about the Kennedys themselves. I’m thrilled to be able to explore this saga of a family and a world in transition with Eric Roth, Thomas Vinterberg and our incredible band of artists and partners, at a moment when our past feels urgently present.”

Here’s the cast of Netflix’s upcoming “Kennedy” series.

Michael Fassbender as Joe Kennedy Sr.

Michael Fassbender attends the “Black Bag” UK Special Screening at the Curzon Mayfair on March 11, 2025, in London, England. (Photo by Samir Hussein/WireImage)

The streaming giant has tapped Academy Award nominee Michael Fassbender to play patriarch Joe Kennedy Sr.

Kennedy Sr. was a businessman and politician who served as the 44th U.S. Ambassador to the United Kingdom.

Laura Donnelly as Rose Kennedy

Laura Donnelly attends the 78th Annual Tony Awards Green Room presented by Cunard at Radio City Music Hall on June 08, 2025 in New York City. (Photo by Jemal Countess/Getty Images for Tony Awards Productions)

Actress Laura Donnelly will play Rose Kennedy, the wife of Kennedy Sr. and the matriarch of the Kennedy dynasty.

Rose Kennedy was an author, activist and philanthropist who inspired her nine children to contribute to society through public service, according to the Rose Kennedy Green Way organization.

Nick Robinson as Joe Kennedy Jr

Nick Robinson attends the premiere of Netflix’s “The Abandons” at Netflix Tudum Theater on December 03, 2025 in Los Angeles, California. (Photo by Leon Bennett/Getty Images)

“Love, Simon” actor Nick Robinson will play Joe Kennedy Jr., the eldest child of Rose Kennedy and Kennedy Sr.

Kennedy Jr. was a lieutenant in the U.S. Navy and a naval aviator who died during World War II.

Joshuah Melnick as Jack Kennedy

NEW YORK, NEW YORK – JUNE 26: Joshuah Melnick attends the “Please Don’t Feed The Children” New York premiere at Village East Cinema on June 26, 2025 in New York City. (Photo by Dia Dipasupil/Getty Images)

In the upcoming Netflix series, Joshuah Melnick will portray Jack Kennedy, known to the world as John F. Kennedy, the 35th president of the U.S. The first season of the show is set in the 1930s, decades before Kennedy entered the White House.

data.title: Newsom says Trump was never going to invade Greenland
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) said President Trump was not going to invade Greenland, comments that followed the president ruling out military force to acquire the island.

“He was never going to invade Greenland,” Newsom told CNN’s Kaitlan Collins while at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. “It was never real. So, that was always a fake and so, he says, ‘Well, we should negotiate.’”

“Well, everybody here has been willing to negotiate for a year,” the Golden State governor added.

During a Wednesday speech at the forum, Trump pushed aside taking over Greenland by force after previously not ruling it out.

“We probably won’t get anything unless I decide to use excessive strength and force where we would be frankly unstoppable. But, I won’t do that,” Trump said. “I don’t have to use force. I don’t want to use force. I won’t use force. All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland.”

Trump’s quest to acquire Greenland, which has ramped up in recent weeks, has strained relationships with American allies including the U.K. and Canada. The Arctic island is a territory of Denmark, which is in the NATO alliance alongside the U.S.

Newsom had slammed world leaders on Tuesday over their responses to Trump’s foreign policy initiatives.

“Do you have a message for Europeans who are concerned about the messages from the White House around Greenland this week?” Sky News’s Paul Kelso asked Newsom.

“Yeah, it’s time to buck up,” Newsom replied. “It’s time to get serious. And stop being complicit. It’s time to stand tall and firm, have a backbone. You know, I’ve seen this in the United States, supine Congress, playing both sides. You know, say one thing on a text or a tweet, another publicly.”

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: House panel to depose Ghislaine Maxwell on Feb. 9, Comer says
data.description:
The House Oversight and Government Reform Committee will depose longtime Jeffrey Epstein associate Ghislaine Maxwell on Feb. 9, Committee Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) said on Wednesday. 

Maxwell was sentenced to 20 years in prison in 2022 for her role in procuring abuse victims for Epstein. 

Comer made the announcement during the committee’s meeting on holding former President Clinton and former Secretary Hillary Clinton in contempt of Congress for failing to appear for depositions after being subpoenaed in the panel’s Epstein probe.

Several Democrats during the hearing pointed to the delay in hearing from Maxwell in explaining their opposition to holding the Clintons in contempt.

Comer had previously said it was unlikely the committee would send lawyers or staff members to meet with Maxwell, who is being held in a Texas prison, because she has said she would plead the fifth in declining to answer questions.

“We need to hear from Ghislaine Maxwell,” Comer said Wednesday. “We’ve been trying to get her bid for a deposition. Our lawyers have been saying that she’s going to plead the fifth, but we have nailed down a date, February 9th, where Ghislaine Maxwell will be deposed by this committee.”

Developing.

data.title: Supreme Court voices hesitation at allowing Trump to fire Fed's Lisa Cook
data.description:
President Trump’s bid to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook faced a hesitant Supreme Court on Wednesday at a blockbuster oral argument over the central bank’s removal protections.  

A majority of the justices appeared reluctant to sign off on Trump’s argument that he could terminate Cook over mortgage fraud accusations, seeming fretful that greenlighting Cook’s firing may wreck the central bank’s independence. 

“We have to be aware of what we’re doing and the consequences of your position for the structure of the government,” said Justice Brett Kavanaugh. 

“There’s a risk,” noted Justice Amy Coney Barrett. 

Trump became the first president to try to fire a sitting Fed governor in the Fed’s 112-year history when he sacked Cook last August. 

It comes as he broadly assails the Fed for not lowering interest rates quicker, saying they should take more aggressive action on lowering borrowing costs to stimulate the economy. 

Wall Street has met Trump’s threats with shrugs. Stock markets have repeatedly notched record highs in recent months under expectations that Trump would face obstacles in trying to forcibly remake the central bank’s board. 

Now, investors and lawyers alike are turning their eyes to the high court as it is thrust into the center of the battle between Trump and the Fed. A decision is expected by July.
  

Cook’s firing came after Federal Housing Finance Agency Director William Pulte referred her to the Justice Department for mortgage fraud, accusing her of listing both her Michigan and Atlanta properties as her primary residences to obtain better financing terms. Cook denies committing any fraud. 

The Federal Reserve Act prevents the president from terminating governors except “for cause,” and all sides agree that a governor’s interest rate votes aren’t a valid reason. But the statute doesn’t explicitly spell out what is included. 

It left the Trump administration and Cook battling Wednesday over what standard to apply and whether the mortgage fraud allegations qualify. 

Solicitor General D. John Sauer, representing Trump, urged the court to adopt a broad standard that covers any cause relating to a Fed governor’s conduct, ability, fitness or competence of the officer. 

Defending Cook’s firing, Sauer said her alleged conduct “sends the wrong message to the market and to the American people who have to make correct representations to the banks.” 

Cook was represented by Paul Clement. A respected conservative lawyer, Clement was then-President George W. Bush’s top Supreme Court advocate. Clement has opposed Trump on several fronts over the past year, including by representing WilmerHale in its fight against Trump’s executive order targeting the law firm.   

Clement urged the court to adopt the removal standard used at a handful of other agencies that requires one of three causes: inefficiency, neglect of duty or malfeasance in office. 

“There is simply no reason to abandon over 100 years of central bank independence on an emergency application on a preliminary record,” Clement said Wednesday. 

That preliminary record gave some of the justices pause, with several suggesting the court may send the case back to the lower courts to air out some of the issues.

“We know that the independence of the agency is very important, and that that independence is harmed if we decide these issues too quickly,” said Justice Sonia Sotomayor. 

It appeared to be a prepared statement. Sotomayor read it from a paper, occasionally looking up and toward her colleagues sitting on the bench alongside her as she spoke.

At another point, Justice Samuel Alito asked why the whole matter had to be handled in “such a hurried manner.” 

“Are the mortgage applications even in the record in this case?” he quipped.

The Supreme Court’s conservative majority has appeared sympathetic to Trump’s broader second-term effort to fire most independent agency leaders at will, part of his expansive vision of presidential power. Trump argues the removal protections infringe on his constitutional authority to oversee the executive branch. 

He has not made that argument for the Fed, which the justices have signaled they view as having special status that may still justify its protections. The Fed is a “uniquely structured, quasi-private entity” that follows the historical tradition of the First and Second Banks of the United States, the court wrote last May. 

It’s that uniqueness that has enabled Cook to remain on the Fed’s board and continue to vote on interest rates as the litigation progresses. 

After she sued, a federal judge agreed that Trump didn’t have proper “cause” because the allegations concern conduct before she took office. The judge also ruled Cook was entitled to due process, including proper notice and a hearing.  A 2-1 appeals panel upheld the ruling on the due process claim, leading Trump to head to the Supreme Court. 

The justices typically resolve emergency cases without oral arguments. But in an unusual move, the justices opted to hold the courtroom session, punting a decision until after they could hold Wednesday’s session.

data.title: AARP calls for accountability over DOGE sharing Social Security data
data.description:
AARP on Wednesday called for staffers at the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to be held accountable for sharing Social Security data with an advocacy group seeking to “overturn election results” and for storing private information on Cloudflare.

“SSA is entrusted with the sensitive data of hundreds of millions of Americans, and protecting that data from illegal use must be a top priority,” Nancy LeaMond, AARP chief advocacy and engagement officer, told The Hill in a statement. 

“Anyone involved must be held accountable and the Social Security Administration must take immediate steps to ensure nothing like this can happen again,” she added.

DOGE staffers stored personal information from 1,000 individuals on Cloudflare, an internet services company, according to court documents.

Two DOGE employees at the Social Security Administration (SSA) were contacted by a political advocacy group that sought to analyze state voter rolls with the goal of finding “evidence of voter fraud and to overturn election results in certain States,” according to the filing. One DOGE employee ultimately signed a “Voter Data Agreement” with the group.

“At this time, there is no evidence that SSA employees outside of the involved members of the DOGE Team were aware of the communications with the advocacy group,” the filing notes. “Nor were they aware of the ‘Voter Data Agreement.’ This agreement was not reviewed or approved through the agency’s data exchange procedures.”

SSA first learned about the agreement “during a review unrelated to this case” in November and made two Hatch Act referrals in late December. The Hatch Act bars federal employees from engaging in political activity.

Democrats on Wednesday also decried the leak and called for DOGE staffers to be held accountable.

“Remember when the Supreme Court gave DOGE the green light to access your social security data? It was never about curtailing waste, fraud, and abuse,” Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) wrote in a statement on the social platform X.

“The DOJ just admitted DOGE was ‘secretly’ in touch with advocacy groups ‘seeking to overturn election results.’ The idea being: use a trove of personal information to disenfranchise voters and change election results,” he added.

Schumer continued, “DOGE accessing your data was illegal. Using it to overturn election results is illegal. We must hold the DOGE staff involved accountable.”

Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.) echoed his concerns.

“A year ago, we stood outside SSA, protesting DOGE’s access for just this reason. The Trump Admin denied it. Once again, the truth comes out: they lied,” Van Hollen posted on X.

“Meanwhile, DOGE’s haphazard firings & disruptions have made life harder for millions of Americans who rely on Social Security.”

data.title: To defeat cartels, the US must confront its own role — not threaten Mexico
data.description:
President Trump justified U.S. military action against Venezuela by claiming it would stem the flow of drugs into the U.S. That intervention did not reduce American drug use, nor did it dismantle transnational criminal networks. Nonetheless, Trump has suggested he could apply the same rationale to Mexico.

If the objective is truly to stop drugs, attacking Mexico would be counterproductive, because military action ignores the central reality of the drug trade: Cartels are fueled by overwhelming U.S. demand and sustained by a steady flow of American-made weapons. If Washington wants fewer drugs on U.S. streets, it should deepen its already successful cooperation with Mexico and take immediate action at home — reducing demand and cutting off the southbound gun pipeline.

Under President Claudia Sheinbaum, Mexican authorities have intensified operations against cartel networks while maintaining close coordination with U.S. agencies. 

The results are undeniable.

According to U.S. officials, including leadership within the Drug Enforcement Administration, drug flows — particularly fentanyl — have dropped sharply:

  • U.S. Customs and Border Protection data cited in reporting show average monthly fentanyl seizures declining from roughly 771 kilograms in 2024 to about 338 kilograms in 2025, a reduction of more than 50 percent year-over-year. Much of this drop has been attributed to joint U.S.-Mexico efforts targeting precursor chemicals and expanding interdiction training.
  • The Drug Enforcement Administration’s 2025 threat reporting notes that many Mexico-based fentanyl producers are struggling to obtain key precursor chemicals — clear evidence that coordinated enforcement is disrupting supply chains for synthetic opioids.

Extraditions of captured cartel leaders have increased, and financial networks tied to organized crime remain under sustained pressure. These gains are not symbolic. They reflect strengthened investigative units, a sharper focus on senior cartel leadership, and faster, more reliable intelligence sharing with U.S. counterparts.

During an official visit to Mexico City in 2025, Secretary of State Marco Rubio acknowledged this progress, stating, “Never before in the history of both countries has there been this level of cooperation — cooperation that respects the sovereignty and integrity of both nations, but at the same time delivers concrete results.”

This unprecedented cooperation would be jeopardized by even limited U.S. military action. An attack would violate Mexican sovereignty and collapse the trust on which intelligence sharing, extraditions, and financial enforcement depend. Treating Mexico as a battlefield would fracture a partnership that is demonstrably working.

Proponents of military escalation also underestimate the risks involved. Cartels are not conventional military targets. They are adaptive, decentralized networks embedded in trade, transportation and financial systems. Faced with U.S. force, they would not confront American troops directly. Instead, they could retaliate asymmetrically — disrupting ports, sabotaging trucking corridors or threatening energy infrastructure.

Mexico is the largest importer of U.S. liquefied natural gas. Any interference with those flows would ripple immediately through North American energy markets and supply chains, raising costs for U.S. consumers and businesses long before criminal organizations were weakened.

There is a deeper strategic danger as well. Military action risks reframing cartels domestically as defenders against foreign aggression. History shows that criminal organizations thrive when state legitimacy erodes. An external strike could strengthen cartel recruitment, undermine public trust in institutions, and destabilize a cooperative government that is delivering results.

But the most glaring omission in calls for force is the United States’ own responsibility.

American demand is the engine of cartel power. Tens of millions of Americans report illicit drug use each year, and the United States accounts for the overwhelming share of global fentanyl consumption. As long as that demand remains largely unchecked, supply will adapt — regardless of how aggressively Washington threatens action abroad.

Equally damaging is the flow of U.S.-sourced weapons into Mexico. These weapons — often high-caliber rifles — allow cartels to challenge the Mexican state, intimidate communities, and outgun local authorities. Yet U.S. efforts to interdict southbound gun trafficking remain under-resourced and politically neglected.

If the administration were serious about weakening cartels, it would expand joint gun-trafficking task forces, increase inspections and prosecutions targeting illegal firearms exports, and invest meaningfully in demand reduction at home — treatment, prevention and harm reduction — rather than threatening a close partner.

The United States does not need to coerce Mexico to defeat the cartels. Mexico is already doing that work — alongside us. The question for Washington is whether it is willing to confront its own role in sustaining the crisis.

If President Trump’s goal is fewer drugs, safer communities and a more secure border, the answer is not escalation. It is shared responsibility: reinforcing cooperation with Mexico, reducing American demand for illegal drugs and stopping U.S.-made weapons from flowing south.

Richard Swett served as the U.S. representative for New Hampshire’s 2nd District from 1991 to 1995. He also served as the U.S. Ambassador to Denmark from 1998 to 2001. Republican Christopher Shays served as the U.S. representative for Connecticut’s 4th District from 1987 to 2009.
